titlePage.titlePart: How will they get out of it?
An Original Comedy, in three acts.
titlePage.byline: by ARTHUR SKETCHLEY, Esq., author of A Dark Cloud, Smiths at Norwood, A Quiet Morning, Paris, and Mrs Brown at the Play, &c &c.

performance.head: How will they get out of it?
performance.p: First produced at the Royal St. James's Theatre (under the management of Mr. Benjamin Webster) on Friday, August 12th, 1864.

set.p: Time in Representation – Two Hours and Five Minutes

div.head: How will they get out of it?
ACT I.
SCENE.—
div.stage: Breakfast Room at Major Oldfield’s Villa; entrance in flat, C., through French window view of Garden and distant Sea; entrances R. and L.; breakfast table R., writing table L., sofa, chairs, &c.
MAJOR and MRS. OLDFIELD discovered; MRS. OLDFIELD at table, R., MAJOR on sofa, L.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: (letter in hand) Well, my love, what’s to be done?

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Done? Whatever you please! The idea of consulting me when you know you are never guided by me.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: The deuce I’m not; every one knows which is the better horse in this team.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Major Oldfield, I must beg you not to be coarse.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: I wish you’d give me your ideas about Ally’s letter. Shall I read it again?

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: No, for I never understand half you say when you do read to me.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: That’s civil! well then, read the letter yourself.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: That is not necessary, tell me what she says?

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: (reading) “Dearest papa and mamma, As you have not answered my letter, I must conclude that you are very, very angry.” Dear girl, I’m sure we’re not.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Pray, Major Oldfield, speak for yourself.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: (reading) “I cannot bear the idea of your displeasure, so am coming home to-morrow; we shall leave by the early train and be with you by lunch time, that is to say if you will receive us. If you do not meet us at the station we shall go on by the train somewhere, and I shall break my heart and—”

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: There that will do, that’s quite enough. Break her heart indeed! She must have a nice tender heart to act as she has done.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: What has she done, but followed the dictates of her heart and married the man she loved? If it had been that precious spoilt boy of yours, master Freddy, you’d have had plenty of excuses for his conduct.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: His conduct never requires excuses. Don’t be cowardly, Major Oldfield, and attack your own son behind his back!

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Oh, I’d attack him often enough to his face, only you won’t let me.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: You are so unjust to him, and seem only to take pleasure in thwarting him.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Thwarting him! why I’ve indulged him in every whim! Didn’t he have a tutor at home instead of going to school? didn’t he prefer Cambridge because I suggested Oxford? and didn’t he go into the army because my heart was set on seeing him in the Church? and now don’t he go moaning and spooning about with Jessy because I have told him that I should particularly object to his marrying her?

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Marrying her indeed! he’s not so silly, dear boy— he’ll do better than that; besides you must indeed be blind if you don’t see there is some one else admires her.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Who’s that pray?

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Percy Wylding.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Percy Wylding, nonsense! he’s a confirmed old bachelor.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: He’s nothing of the sort. I’ll find out his sentiments at any rate, and then perhaps you’ll leave off persecuting poor dear Freddy; a better son never lived.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Yes, he can coax and wheedle you out of anything.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: He’d never stoop to any such meanness as coaxing and wheedling!

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Wouldn’t he? Pray how did he get the money to extricate himself from that last little difficulty?

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Major Oldfield, young men will be young men—I’m sure you needn’t hold yourself up as a model, for—

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Confound it, Mrs. Oldfield, am I never to hear the last of your lectures on my past conduct? But come, what’s to be done with regard to Alice ?

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: I really can’t say. Consult our friend Percy.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: With all my heart; in fact I wrote him a line enclosing Ally’s letter, and asked him to come and talk it over; he returned it saying he’d be here very soon.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: You might have saved yourself the trouble, for he is coming to be introduced to our friend Mrs. Tiverton.

div.stage: Enter JESSY ASHTON, C., with flowers..

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Ah, Jessy!

sp.speaker: Jessy.
sp.p: Good morning, dear aunt. (kisses her) Aren’t they lovely? (gives flowers)

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Thanks, my love, they are indeed.

sp.speaker: Jessy.
sp.p: Good morning, uncle there’s a rose for you.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Thank’ee, my dear; I know which I admire most.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Did you knock at Mrs. Tiverton’s door, my love?

sp.speaker: Jessy.
sp.p: No occasion, aunt she’s in the garden and will be here directly.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Good morning—I fear I’m very late.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: (rises) Good morning—Not at all for a traveller.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: (coming forward to meet her) You ladies are always privileged to be late. Mrs. Oldfield is not a very early bird. I am! I had my breakfast with Jessy at eight o’clock! You’re not the last, there’s that lazy young dog my son down yet.

sp.speaker: Jessy.
sp.p: Oh, yes, he is, uncle, he’s been in the garden this half hour –(checks herself)

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: (aside) Dangling after her as usual. (aloud) I’m glad to hear it, my love, though I must say—

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Major Oldfield, hand Mrs. Tiverton her tea. MAJOR goes to table, R. I hope you slept well.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Famously, thank you. What a charming place you have here; no wonder you were so little inclined to stay abroad last year.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: It’s a nice little place, isn’t it?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Little place, indeed! why it’s quite a family house. I really think if I stayed here long I should be tempted to settle in the country.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Not you; the country is very well for old-fashioned humdrum folks like ourselves, but would never suit you, calculated as you are to adorn society.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Major Oldfield, when you’ve done being fulsome, perhaps you’ll give Mrs. Tiverton some breakfast.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Certainly, my love, certainly. What will you take?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Nothing in the way of solids, thank you. I’m a poor hand at breakfast.

div.stage: Enter FREDDY, C., goes to MRS. OLDFIELD—MAJOR retires, L.

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.p: Good morning.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: You naughty boy, to be so late.

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.p: I didn’t think any one would be down before this; it is so very early. I’m sure, Mrs. Tiverton, you don’t approve of such heathenish hours.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Don’t be a puppy, sir. It’s nearly eleven o’clock, which is late enough, I’m sure, even for your breakfast.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Do you return to your charming chalet at Lausanne, Mrs. Tiverton?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p:
No; I seldom go anywhere twice, besides I was only there for quiet. I live but for excitement, and love
change. I’m only in England for a few weeks; I came on business, and except for the sake of seeing a few kind friends like yourselves, I shouldn’t have stayed beyond a day or two.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Wouldn’t you? Why, I should have thought the London season just your sphere of action.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: I’ve had enough of it. It’s all very well for young girls during the first season or two. By the way, I hope your daughter will be able to tear herself away from town before my visit is over. When I saw her there, she told me she would be sure to be at home by the time of my visit.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Young ladies are not much to be relied on.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: I don’t wonder at her staying, for she is so much admired that I don’t believe she’ll be allowed to leave. I thought her charming last year when we met in Germany, but now she is dazzling, and in such spirits. If I were a man, I’m sure I would run away with her.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: I am sorry to say, Mrs. Tiverton, that’s what a man has done.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Major Oldfield!

sp.speaker: Jessy.
sp.p: Uncle, how can you?

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.p: Come, governor, that’s too strong!

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: That’s right, attack me on all sides—what else can you call a clandestine marriage?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: I’m sure I’m very sorry that I—

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Major Oldfield, you outrage decency! The fact is, Mrs. Tiverton, our daughter has formed a hasty—I may say, an indiscreet—but not a clandestine marriage, for she was married from her aunt’s house, with her brother’s sanction, and in his presence.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: That’s a very tame affair. I was in hopes I was going to hear an interesting account of some desperate run-away love-match. I do delight in such things. I always advise all the young people I know to fall in love.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Ha! ha! deuced good!

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Major Oldfield! (frowns at him and rises) Jessy, my love, will you tell Edwards I shall want the carriage early.

sp.speaker: Jessy.
sp.p: Yes, aunt.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Feed my birds for me this morning, that’s a dear. Exit JESSY, R. Freddy, you go into the garden and smoke your cigar. I know you’re all impatience to do so.

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.p: All right, dear mother.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: (resumes her seat) Excuse, me Mrs. Tiverton, but really Major Oldfield is so very imprudent before young people.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: I like that—why what did I say?

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Never mind! If we are not troubling Mrs. Tiverton with our family affairs, we might ask her advice.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: I’m sure I should be most interested in anything that concerns you.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: You’re very kind. Well then, you see, our daughter went about six weeks ago to spend a short time with her aunt, contrary to my judgment.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Pray, my dear, don’t bore Mrs. Tiverton with tiresome details; she knows that Alice was on a visit to her aunt; and not to detain her any longer, our daughter has thought fit to marry, after an acquaintance of three weeks !

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: That’s rather a long courtship in my opinion! Why I ran away before I was seventeen, with a young man I’d only met at two county balls!

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Did you though? Was it a happy match?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: (aside) The less I say about that the better. (aloud) Happy as the day was long while it lasted.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: You have been sadly tried in losing him ?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Oh dear no—not at all—that is, I mean, of course, only –– I’m resigned.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Widowhood must be a great trial. I’ve often thought if I were permitted to be a widow—

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Indeed! what would you do?

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: I can’t say, but I should always respect the memory of the departed.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Well, I’ve never thought of what I should do if I—it’s not worth while, for I see little chance of any practical experience on the point.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Major Oldfield, how can you say such grossly indelicate things?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Come, come; what is the point on which you were going to ask my advice? (they rise)

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Why, our daughter!

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Dear me, how tiresome you are! let Mrs. Tiverton read Alice’s letter.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: By all means. (hands letter to MRS. TIVERTON and crosses round to R.)

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: (reads) Yes, very nice, very natural. Of course you meet her—at least I should say so.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: That’s what I say.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: It’s what I should have wished, only Major Oldfield makes it a matter of principle to differ with me.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: My love, didn’t we agree to refer the question to Percy?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Who’s Percy?

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Our next door neighbour, I may say our family guide, Mrs. Tiverton, he’s most anxious to make your acquaintance.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p:
(crosses to c.) You’ll be delighted with him, such a
capital fellow, you’ll suit each other to a nicety; he’s full of fun, a heart as large as the dome of St. Paul’s, a little eccentric —his cottage adjoins ours, such a doll’s house and such a lovely lawn; you’ll see it to-morrow as we’re going to lunch with him.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: But who is he?

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Why Percy ––PERCY WYLDING is heard speaking without, c. Ah, there he is!

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: (without) What? Indoors such a morning as this! I’ll soon have them out!

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: That voice! Can it be possible?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: I never did in my life know such people, why you’re wasting the best hours of our lives.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: We were waiting for you; how are you?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Waiting for me! Couldn’t you wait out of doors?

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Good morning, Mr. Wylding, allow me to introduce to you our friend Mrs. Tiverton—Mr. Wylding. PERCY crosses to MRS. TIVERTON

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: I’m sure I’m delighted. I hope I’ve the pleasure of seeing you well. (crosses to MRS. TIVERTON, who turns and looks at him, curtseying very low, PERCY starts back—aside) Whew! Here’s a go! What’s to be done?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: It gives me great pleasure to meet Mr. Wylding!

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: You’re old acquaintances. eh?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Why yes—well, not exactly – that is – (aside to MRS. TIVERTON) What’s to be done, eh?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: (aside to him) You take your line, I take mine. (aloud) Yes, Mr. Wylding and I have met before. I was a little taken by surprise at seeing him here as I did not know you were acquainted.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: (aside) I thought I was cool, but she’s a refrigerator. (aloud) Yes—yes, it’s deuced funny. You’re quite well I hope, my dear—I mean, I beg your pardon, my good—no, that is–– (aside) What can a man say when he meets his wife under such circumstances?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Mr. Wylding, I hear that you are settled here— not married I suppose.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: (aside) What a question? How can you? (aloud) No, that is not exactly—I mean– I really don’t know what I mean!

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: My dear Wylding, I’m sorry to interrupt any pleasant little passages between you and your friend Mrs. Tiverton, but we want your advice on the subject of Ally’s letter.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Oh, that’s to be ventilated in public, is it?

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: We consider Mrs. Tiverton quite an intimate friend.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Why there can’t be two opinions about it—of course you’ll go and meet her.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Of course you wouldn’t refuse your own child!

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Impossible!

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: You must be a flinty-hearted old monster if you could even think of such a thing.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: It’s been my wish all along to do so, only Mrs. Old- field seems to hang back.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: I seem to hang back! Major Oldfield, how can you—

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Yes, how can you? I know very well what you are— a regular tyrant—a bashaw! You’re too lenient with him, Mrs. Oldfield, that’s your fault.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: I’m afraid it is. I try to struggle against my weakness.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: No doubt. (aside) Her weakness? that must be the Major, for her life is a constant struggle against him.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Pray who is your son-in-law? does any one know him?

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Oh, he’s a gentleman in every respect, and I hear good accounts of him.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: A man of fortune?

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: His position is all that we could wish.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Then it’s a subject of congratulation after all!

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Of course it is; Mrs. Oldfield very naturally regrets having been spared all the trouble of preparing the trousseau— can’t bear not having the house turned upside down for a month, in honour of the wedding. Speaking selfishly, I’m delighted the young couple stole away, for I know what an affair the wedding breakfast would have been. I tremble to think of the discomfort of new clothes—food all cold and sticky—then the speeches—all the girls crying—the Major sobbing like an infant, and Mrs. Oldfield in hysterics, with a glass of champagne thrown over her new glacè silk. No, no, depend on it it’s the best as it is, and we ought to be very thankful, oughtn’t we, my dear—I mean Mrs. Tiverton? so come, Major, be off to the train—receive the young couple with open arms, and we’ll all be happy.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: (looking at watch) Yes, it’s time you were gone. I’ve ordered the carriage to be ready. (rings)

sp.speaker: Servant.
sp.p: The carriage is ready. (he removes breakfast things)

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Well then, I’m off, and only too happy. I shall find you here when I return, Wylding—you must detain him, Mrs. Tiverton.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: That is a task far beyond my powers,

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: (aside) How can you say so? (aloud) Never fear, I shall be somewhere near at hand, so toddle off, Major.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: I must ask to be excused for a little time, Mrs. Tiverton, as I have some household arrangements to make. As to Mr. Wylding, he’s at home.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Pray make no stranger of me, or I shall not be able to make myself as comfortable as I could wish.

div.stage: Exit MRS. OLDFIELD, R. door. —a pause: PERCY and MRS. TIVERTON look at each other.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Certainly this looks like destiny.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Destiny! of course it is – there’s no escaping it. Well, what’s to be done: shall I go off summoned by telegraph to see an uncle from whom I’ve great expectations, or will you discover that the place don’t agree with you?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
Percy.
sp.p: I’m sure through no fault of mine.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Whose fault was it, then? I took to travelling as the surest way of avoiding you, knowing what a confirmed stay-at-home you were.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: I was forced to travel when you left me, in order to distract my thoughts.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Having me no longer to distract with your whims and fancies.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: My dear Harriet —I mean Mrs. Tiverton—let us not refer to the past. May I venture to enquire why you changed your name?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: How could I go about as your widow, when every one knows you to be alive; besides, the bore of being asked, “Are you Mrs. Percy Wylding, of Macclesfield?” Was I not always meeting you? Why our luggage was put in the same room at Berlin?—

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Very true; it was awkward certainly, and I think you have acted wisely. Your plan of action then, under existing circumstances, is to maintain your position. Very well, I’ll maintain mine. We are merely acquaintances, and solemnly pledge ourselves not to divulge our secret.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: By all means.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Widowhood seems to agree with you as well as bachelor-life does with me. What truly sensible people we’ve shewn ourselves to be.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Yes; I flatter myself I displayed no small degree of worldly wisdom when I suggested our separation.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: You suggested it, I like that. Why I insisted on it: don’t you remember the dispute we had about whether the breakfast room was to be paper or chintz?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Pray, sir, don’t remind me of the outrage on a woman’s feelings of which you were guilty. When you wished me to have the drawing room amber, when I had selected blue.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: I’m sure it was about the breakfast room we differed, but I’ll throw the drawing room in if you like. I know you drove me from the house, and that I wrote you a note expressive of my wish that we might never meet again. (they rise)

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: I beg your pardon: I went down to Oatlands, with my dear Aunt Fussell, and sent you a note to say that, as far as I was concerned you might break up our establishment.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: I took you at your word and sold everything.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Hadn’t the delicacy of feeling even to reserve our wedding presents.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: I didn’t wish to be reminded of the blight of my existence. I had done all I could to make you happy, and had failed.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: You certainly failed; is it possible you can forget how your impetuosity and blundering caused me increasing discomfort? why, you married me before I knew where I was, and separated from me in a still greater hurry.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: My impetuosity? I’m the coolest man alive: Blundering indeed! why every one relies upon my judgment but you.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Pray, how much mischief have you done here with your hasty conclusions and thoughtless acts? Didn’t you break off more marriages and give rise to more false reports—unintentionally I admit—than any other man in society?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: How can I help other people being fools, they either tell me half their stories, or make some blunder which misleads me, you yourself were the cause of nearly all the difficulties by telling me only part of what you heard.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Mr. Wylding, allow me to observe we are treading on dangerous ground. We are separated—1 am happy, so are you—we’ll not make ourselves the talk of this place, therefore not a word. A week is a short time for us to practise the self-denial of meeting and not quarrelling, so let us be friends externally at any rate, and forget we were ever man and wife. (cross to R.) Good morning, Mr. Wylding; we meet at dinner 1 believe—good morning!

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p:
Good morning. Gad, she’s a wonder! to think of our meeting in this manner after being separated for more than three years; well, this is the end of a love match. How I doted on her from our first meeting till we were married—it
was only ten days to be sure; we were no sooner man and wife than we discovered that we hadn’t a taste or sentiment in common. I love the country, she detests it, will be in London all the season, five parties a night, to say nothing of the Opera; when that was over, I naturally hoped for quiet; but no, her only taste was the baths of Homburg, Baden Baden, early winter in Vienna, or St. Petersburg, and Paris in the spring. Then, we never could agree as to where to reside, or even as having a window open or shut; at last we came to an open rupture, and parted; it was but to meet again, for wherever I went I was sure to fall in with her. I do believe if I went up into the ball at the top of St. Paul’s, she’d be there; and if I went down in a diving bell, she’d be the other passenger. Once I found myself tete-a-tete with her in a railway carriage; but as she read perseveringly, and I slept, we got on very well to the next station. I was set down to cards with her at the English Embassy at Vienna, and we met twice on the Swiss Lakes; so I gave up travelling, and betook myself to a rustic life, settled down here, and was as I thought safe from her; when after a little breathing time, here’s my wife again. Well, what’s to be done? I suppose she’s right, we had better fight it out, retreat on either side would only excite suspicion.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: My dear Mr. Wylding, I am so glad to find you alone, for I want to talk to you about a little scheme I have in my head.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: I’m all attention.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Why, I’m rather in a little bit of a difficulty about Freddy.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: What, that boy again in a scrape, he’s incorrigible.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Well, no—not in a scrape—but I’m rather puzzled with respect to him and his cousin Jessy. I don’t quite see my way in that quarter, and I’m naturally anxious.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Yes yes. I see it all.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: The truth is, the major has a very strong objection to Jessy as a daughter-in-law, because he thinks that the world will say he planned the marriage in order to get Jessy’s money into his family.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Jessy has money then?

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Why you knew that, didn’t you?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Never heard of it till now.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Her father left her twenty thousand pounds; and as the major is her guardian and one of her trustees, that’s the reason he sets his face against this match. As for me—

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p:
I quite understand. (aside) The prudent mamma
wishes to bring the match about without seeming to have any hand in it.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Now, seriously, what is your opinion of Jessy? that’s what I want to know.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Charming girl. I quite love her.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: I knew you did. (aside) I told the major so, and I was right. (aloud) Don’t you think she’ll make a capital wife?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: First-rate, and such a fortune! Is it settled on herself?

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: (aside) How keen he seems after the money. (aloud) Yes; but that could be arranged. I’ve no doubt part would be paid over to her husband.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Then I’ll soon manage it. Consider it as a settled point, as far as I am concerned. I quite understand the delicacy of your position.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: A thousand thanks; but, my dear Mr. Wylding, will you let me say I hope there may be no long courtship; 1 do hate that sort of thing.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: So do I; I always like to strike while the iron’s hot. Two people mooning and spooning about, wishing everybody else anywhere out of their way; and such a bore when you come on them suddenly, it’s so deuced awkward. I’m sure I’m the last man to encourage any such folly—sharp’s the word.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: I know that I may leave everything to your taste and judgment.

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.p: (without, c.) Good-bye.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: You have relieved my mind: many thanks; there’s Freddy coming: I’ll not meet him just yet: he must not think I’ve had any hand in the affair—so good-bye.

div.stage: Enter FREDDY, C., kissing his hand, down, L.

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.p: Good-bye, dear girl, good-bye.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Hallo! What are you up to?

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.p: My dear friend, congratulate me I know you will— I’ve just told my love to dear Jessy, and she’s accepted me.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: She has! Well you’re a cool hand—what will the major say, and your mother?

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.p: Oh, I expect all sorts of opposition, but must outlive that. Jessy will be of age in six months, and we will then marry in spite of guardians.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: You will, will you—then you’re a nice wilful pair— what dou mean by enticing a pretty girl into wedlock? I’m ashamed of you.

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.p: But, my dear Percy, I’m sure I may rely on your good offices with my parents.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: That you may, I can tell you as far as your mother goes, you’re all right there.

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.p: You don’t mean it. Why she has been pestering me to death about the wealthy Miss Potts, the drysalter’s daughter.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Nonsense, your mother wishes you to marry Jessy. Now, I know it –don’t interrupt me—so you’ve nothing to do but to get married as soon as you can.

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.p: No! you are not serious?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: I am. Your mother has told me all about it, in confidence—not a word– your father would make a show of displeasure to save appearances; so my advice is that you go off quietly and get married on the sly—don’t interrupt me, my boy—I’ll manage it all with your parents—you won’t betray me?

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.p: Betray you! what do you mean?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Why, don’t say I told you; the truth is that both your parents wish the match. They can’t say so; and I’ve undertaken to manage the affair.

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.p: Jessy will never consent to an elopement.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Leave her to me; you are all coming to lunch with me to-morrow; and I’ll talk to her. Consider it as a settled thing at any rate with your father and mother.

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.p: Well, if you were not so positive, I should say you were mistaken.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Mistaken, indeed! I’m never mistaken; I never made a mistake in my life; I’m sharp, but sure; but I hear the carriage—it’s the happy pair I’ll be off till the first interview is over with mamma.

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.p: I shall cut that, and go and find Jesse, and tell her my news.

div.stage: Enter MAJOR OLDFIELD, ALICE, and EGERTON, C.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Here we are welcome home, my love. (kisses her) Glad to see you, Egerton. (shakes hands)

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Thanks, dear papa. Harry, why don’t you say “Thank you.” How dull you seem.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: My dearest love, I’m sure I’ve expressed my thanks.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Then do be a little lively, that’s a dear, else mamma’ll think I’ve married a mute. Where is dear mamma?

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: She’ll be here directly, no doubt. Ah! I said so!

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: (running to her and embracing her) Dear, dear mamma, I’m so pleased to see you. How well you look! You’re glad to see Ally, I know.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Ah, Alice, how d’ye do? how d’ye do, Mr. Egerton?

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Mr. Egerton, dear mamma! Don’t look so coolly on me, please don’t; you mustn’t be cross with Ally now that she has come to see you and brought her husband! Harry, come and kiss mamma and say you're a good boy now, and will never do it again.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Dear girl, how she runs on.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Pray, Major Oldfield, don’t interrupt me when I’m going to speak to Alice and her husband—I’m sure I’m glad to see them.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Well, you look very cordial, I must say! My dear, Ally must want some lunch.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: It will be ready almost immediately.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Now, dear mamma is going to be very kind, I know she is! Harry, why don’t you come and talk to mamma?

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: My love, I’ve not had a chance of talking yet. (crosses to MRS. OLDFIELD) I’m most grateful for the very kind reception I have experienced.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Pray, sir, let bygones be bygones, though I must say—

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: My dear, let bygones—

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Don’t interfere, Major Oldfield, EGERTON crosses to R. C. when I am merely about to remark—

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Mamma, mamma! you’re going to be angry, I know you are. You mustn’t be cross with any one but me, and when we’re alone you may beat me if you please, like a kind, dear mamma as you are.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: My dear Alice—

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: No, not Alice, but your own naughty spoilt Ally. Come, pray forgive me.

sp.speaker: Jessy.
sp.p: (running to ALICE) Dear Alice, I’m so glad to see you home again! (both FREDDY and she shake hands with EGERTON—FREDDY embraces his sister)

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: (aside) Well, it’s all over; mamma don’t look over pleased. (crosses to ALICE) Ah, my dear Mrs. Egerton, I’m delighted to congratulate you.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Many thanks. Mrs. Tiverton, allow me to introduce my husband.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Most happy to make his acquaintance. Ah, Mr. Egerton! is it possible?

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: (aside) Not a word now, I’ll explain all by and bye. (aloud) I’m very happy, Mrs. Tiverton, to ––(hesitates)

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: (aside) So much for man’s constancy!

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: You know each other, then?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Yes—that is –we’ve met before.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: (makes signs to MRS. TIVERTON, observed by ALICE) Yes—Mrs. Tiverton and I know each other—that is, we— (aside to MRS. TIVERTON) oblige me by not referring in any way to the past.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: (to him) By all means, if you wish it.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: (aside) How very odd Harry’s manner is!

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Ah, Alice, my dear—I mean Mrs. Egerton, how d’ye do? I’m delighted to see you! wish you joy! (kisses her)

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: He’s mighty cool!

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: (aside) He’s much fonder of other people’s wives than he is of his own.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Thank you, Mr. Wylding. Harry, this is our dear friend, Mr. Wylding.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Delighted to see you.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Thank you. (aside) Where have I seen him before?

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: (aside to WYLDING) Dear Mr. Wylding, do make mamma come round—she’s so cold.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: All right. Wish you joy, major, and you, Mrs. Oldfield. This is jolly to have Alice with us again!

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Thank you, Mr. Wylding.

sp.speaker: Servant.
sp.p: Lunch is ready.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Come along, I’m as hungry as a hunter! and so you must be, Mrs. Tiverton, for you had no breakfast. (takes MRS. T., JESSY and FREDDY go together L.—EGERTON hesitates)

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Mr. Wylding, take Harry in to lunch; mamma and I will follow directly.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: All right. Come along, Mr. Egerton.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Dear mamma, let us understand one another. I have come here to ask you to forgive me for the only act of disrespect towards you that I have ever intentionally committed. Is it your intention to keep up this rigid demeanour towards me?

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: My dear Alice, you must feel that I have reason for being displeased.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Yes, yes, abundant reason. I do not appeal to your reason, but to your own kind heart; 1 have been rash, but I believe I have done wisely. Oh, mother, Egerton is the noblest, finest of natures he loves me to devotion! I could not be deaf to the entreaties of such a passion as his. I ought to have reflected; but, my own dear mamma, it is all your fault. I have been brought up in such a loving manner that my heart was like tinder, ready to ignite at the first flash of affection. Now, dear—dear mamma, you will forgive your own Ally— (falls on her neck) —won’t you– won’t you –won’t you?

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p:
(embracing her) Well, I suppose I must. It’s my
own fault for having spoilt you all your life. (kisses her) There now we’ll go to lunch.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: My dear Alice, your father has sent me to fetch you.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: It’s all right, dear Harry, mamma has forgiven us.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Mrs. Oldfield, I cannot express my gratitude.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: We’ll say no more about it. Come along to lunch. (going, L.)

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Alice, love, I’ll keep our promise to your aunt, and write her a few lines. I’ll follow immediately.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Do, dearest; she’ll be anxious to hear how we got on.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Come, Ally.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Coming, dear mamma.

div.stage: EGERTON seats himself at table, L., unlocking his desk.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: I don’t know why, but I feel almost like a criminal; this happy home recalls scenes which bring sadness to my soul. Why did I not tell Alice the truth? I am, indeed, happy. I love her as devotedly as I ever loved; and yet, at times, a sweet face appears to cast a glance of reproach at me, and the voice of her I once so fondly loved seems to say, “And is this really man’s affection?” (takes out papers, a miniature falls out) Ah, as if to realize my dream, her image comes before me. Surely, it is no treason to my Alice to gaze again on those once loved features. (opens miniature) Dearly loved one. (kisses it, and buries his head in his hands)

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: What a time you’ve been, Harry. EGERTON starts up You’ve written quite enough, I’m sure. Why, what’s the matter? you are in tears.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Nothing, love.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Have you written the note for me?

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: No, I’ve been—I’ll do it at once.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Harry, there is something in that desk that has made you sad—what is it? I must and will see what it is. I shall look through your papers, sir, and destroy everything that is likely to make you melancholy. (goes to desk)

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Alice, don’t touch anything there, I entreat you.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Entreat! What can you have there unfit to meet the eye of your wife? Is this the perfect confidence which you say is the mainspring of happiness between man and wife? You’ve a secret; I’ll find it out.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: No! no! Alice, there is nothing.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Well, then, let me look at the desk.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: No, no, not now—let me write the note.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: You are evading me. What is there in this desk?

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Nothing whatever. (as EGERTON arranges papers the miniature falls over on the floor)

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Ah, a woman’s portrait! (rushes forward to pick it —EGERTON holds her back)

div.head: SCENE.—
div.stage: The Lawn before Percy Wylding’s Cottage; Distant Sea View; terrace and rockwork; Cottage, L., entrance and window practicable—Rustic chairs and tables.
MAJOR and MRS. OLDFIELD, MRS. TIVERTON, MR. and MRS. EGERTON, JESSY and FREDDY, and PERCY WYLDING discovered with croquet sticks.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Ha! ha! capital; but you don’t mean to say, Percy, that he married her?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Married her! of course he did.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Poor fellow! I dare say she bullied him to death. I remember a young fellow who made a hasty marriage, and caught a tartar.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Major Oldfield, pray don’t use such revolting language; you are quite forgetting yourself before our friends.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: She certainly was a lovely creature—wasn’t she, my dear?

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Don’t appeal to me about such a person.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: I forgot you women never could forgive her for being the prettiest woman in the regiment.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: She was nothing of the sort. I, in common with all the ladies, could not tolerate a person who, after neglecting and thwarting her husband in every way, left him under pretence of illness, and returned to England.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: But, my love, all wives are not such models as you.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Well said, papa. (aside) I’m afraid it sounded very like irony. (aloud) I always tell Harry what perfect sympathy exists between you, and that mamma has held the mastery of your heart from the moment you saw her.

div.stage: FREDDY and JESSY, who have been at back, retire up the rocks.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: (aside) She’s not confined her ruling powers to his heart, I should say.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Ah! first love is the only true and lasting love, isn’t it, Harry?

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Why that depends—yes, I mean, of course it is.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Don’t you think it possible to love twice, Mr. Egerton?

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: (aside to her) Don’t torture me. (aloud) My dear Alice, you and I have so often agreed on the point, that we need hardly discuss it.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: We have indeed.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Yes; first love is a first-rate thing as long as it lasts, but if it grows threadbare, why it must be laid aside.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Quite right. I think that when people cease to agree they’re better apart.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Major Oldfield, what monstrous things you say. I’ll appeal to Mrs. Tiverton—can anything justify a woman separating herself from her husband.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: (aside) That’s an awkward thrust.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Well, I’m not sure that, under some extreme— very extreme –circumstances, a woman might not be justified. What say you, Mr. Wylding.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: It’s a conceivable case certainly. (aside to MRS. TIVERTON) I think you’d better leave it alone.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: (aside to PERCY) Depend upon it, I can hold my own. (aloud) Fancy a woman tied to a man with whom she could not support life.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: That would be insupportable, indeed.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Major Oldfield, your known laxity of principle makes you defend any conduct, however incorrect. I blush for you. I consider a man and wife separated as an insult to society. I’m sure Mrs. Tiverton will agree with me.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Come, I say; I didn’t invite you here to have a lecture on the duties of married life; but if we are to have it, by all means let the young couple have the benefit of it. Mrs. Egerton, you must consider this lecture a matrimonial affair, an admonition to persons just entering that estate.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: I’m quite ready to be advised; in fact I want to ask an opinion: Is a man justified in having a secret from his wife?

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: (to ALICE). My love, don’t refer to that subject now.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Certainly not.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Is a woman entitled to withhold anything from her husband?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: That depends on circumstances.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: A wife may withhold disagreeable matters from her husband, may she not, Mrs. Egerton?

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: I can imagine a wife doing so from a good motive, but in my opinion, a mistaken one.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Pray, does not that remark apply equally to the case of a husband?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: A husband has the right to exercise his discretion.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: I protest against that view of the matter, unless in all cases he trusts to his wife’s discretion.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: But if he trusts to her discretion, he must also trust to her indiscretion?

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: You are hard on the ladies, Mr. Wylding; but in my opinion, a wife is best judge as to what her husband should know.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: That’s what a man is as regards his wife.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: I intend to know everything about my husband’s affairs.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Quite right, Mrs Egerton; but don’t carry the spirit of inquiry too far, or you may make awkward discoveries.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Egad, you may; for I remember once a lady who—

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Major Oldfield, I must insist on your keeping your experiences of life for the ears of others.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Confound it, I never can open my lips.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Augustus! Augustus! (rising and going up with MAJOR) I wish to say a word to you.

div.stage: Enter FREDDY, R. U. E., down rocks.

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.p: Oh, come up to the rocks, there’s a match between two yachts.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Alice, won’t you come and see it?

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Not just now, my head aches.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: The fresh air would do you good; it is so delightful, I shall climb up to the highest point to enjoy it.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Alice, I wish you’d come.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Not at present, Harry; you go and assist Mrs. Tiverton.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Oh dear, no; don’t mind me; I can manage very well.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Harry, why don’t you offer Mrs. Tiverton your arm?

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: By all means. Exit with MRS. TIVERTON, R. U. E.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: I must have my doubts settled one way or another That miniature, it was of a young and lovely woman; from the glance I got of it, it struck me it was like Mrs. Tiverton; I’m almost certain of it; it must be—but yet —Mr. Wylding, I want to speak to you..

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: (coming down, c.) To me—by all means.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: It may seem rather odd, but I want you to tell me what you think of my husband.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Precious odd! Think of him! Why, he’s a capital fellow; he’s a little bit serious at times, but that’s nothing; I’m serious myself.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p:
It is about that very seriousness that I want to speak. He appears to love me devotedly, yet I feel that I do
not possess his entire confidence. May I ask a favour of you? It may seem a strange request, but will you observe him closely, and let me know what you think of his behaviour?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: My dear Mrs. Egerton, I really couldn’t undertake such a task.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Pray, do, for I have reason to be distrustful. Have you observed his behaviour with Mrs. Tiverton?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: What do you mean?

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: They have met before; that they acknowledge, but I can’t get Harry to say much about their former acquaintance, and Mrs. Tiverton pointedly avoids the subject, but I suspect—

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: No, no, my dear, you must not suspect. (aside) Yet, I don’t know why she shouldn’t.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Well, it doesn’t amount to suspicion, but I am curious to know when and where he knew Mrs. Tiverton. She’s a dangerous woman.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Oh, dear, no!

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: She’s an attractive one, and that is the same thing. You men are such clever fellows, so grand and mighty, ruling the world and all that sort of thing, and yet if a poor weak woman lay out her nets to catch you, there you are down on your knees, her most devoted slave.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: If this is your view founded on experience, why do you feel any doubt about Egerton?

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: It is not exactly doubt, but a desire to discover whether he has been given to flirtations. I fancy him the type of constancy. I suppose I’m jealous at the thought of his ever having loved any one else. My idea of happiness is, to be the sole object of a man’s affections.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Well, I think second love is very like a fire made of cinders, there is little real warmth in it.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Now I fancy Mrs. Tiverton is an old flame of his.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Oh dear, no! (aside) Yet I don’t see why she may not be.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: I want you to watch them, and tell me what you think.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: (aside) Well, that’s a pleasant task for me. (aloud) But if you have any misgiving on the point, why did you pack them off together to the rocks?

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: In order to give you an opportunity to observe their conduct. I must say, her frankness of manner gives me confidence, but I’m getting very suspicious. I shall go into the house for a little while, and keep quiet, for I have a head-ache. Mind you keep watch for me, and strict watch too. Exit, L., into cottage.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p:
Oh, I’ll watch, trust me! Well, this is an agreeable affair, certainly; if she were not my wife I should not be justified in such a course, but a man may keep his eye on
his wife, and I certainly don’t quite like Harriet’s way of going on – it is clear she don’t miss or regret me much, and may have beguiled her solitude by flirtation with Egerton—but no, it can’t be ––she is a very fascinating woman. How remarkably well she looks to-day—and yet, it’s not likely that a man situated as Egerton is would take up an affair of that sort; besides, hang it all, she’s my wife; I must look out sharp !—Ah, Jessy!

div.stage: Enter JESSY, R. U. E., from rocks.

sp.speaker: Jessy.
sp.p: Oh, Mr. Wylding, I’m so glad I’ve found you; Freddy has told me all that you’ve said. I can hardly believe that you are serious.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: I am quite serious; now you come for a turn with me in the garden, and I’ll tell you all about it. You must be guided by me.

sp.speaker: Jessy.
sp.p: That I will, readily, though I can’t consent to an elopement, that’s too much.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: It’s nothing when you’re used to it!

sp.speaker: Jessy.
sp.p: Mr. Wylding, how can you ?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: I didn’t mean that—no, I meant you’d soon get used to it. Come into the garden, we’ll discuss the matter coolly. Exeunt, R. 3. Ε.

div.stage: MAJOR and MRS. OLDFIELD come forward from R.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: There, Major Oldfield, will you believe your own senses? Did you ever see more perfect billing and cooing?

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Well, to me it looks much more as though he were reading a lecture.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: You know nothing of love making, how should you?

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: How, indeed! it’s so long since I tried my hand at it; not that I’ve forgotten it, either, for though we do disagree on little points, m dear. I’m sure it’s from no want of love. We’re quite specimens of matrimonial happiness!

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: I try to make you happy, Augustus.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: You quite succeed.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: (aside—going) I’ll follow the young people, and give an approving smile to the match I’ve set my heart on. Exit, R. 3 E.

div.stage: Enter MRS. TIVERTON from rocks, R. U. E.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Why, what has become of everybody?

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: This is Liberty Hall, every one does as he pleases here, including the master! isn’t he a nice fellow?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Very agreeable!

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Such taste, too—isn’t this cottage perfection?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Very beautifully situated, but one misses the hand of a lady about it,

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: That’s precisely what I say to him every time we discuss his home arrangements.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: What does he say?

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Evades the subject. I always fancy that there has been something very disagreeable in the matrimonial quarter.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: What do you mean? is he married?

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Why, Mrs. Oldfield fancies so, though he never alludes to the subject; she has an idea that he married some old dragon for money.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Then, Mrs. Oldfield is wrong, for he did nothing of the sort.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Then you knew his late wife?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: I said nothing of the kind, I only was sure Mr. Wylding would be incapable of anything so contemptible as marrying an old dragon for her money.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Bravo! I’m glad you take his part. Well, between you and me, I think he’s getting out of his bachelor ways.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Indeed! How?

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Look there (pointing off R. 3 E.) What do you see?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Mr. Wylding, walking with Miss Ashton.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Yes, and talking very earnestly too, pleading his cause very ardently; at least, so Mrs. Oldfield says.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: He’s talking to her, but not making love. (aside) I don’t see though, what business he has to be talking to her at all. (aloud) No, no; Mr. Wylding has too matured a taste to be caught by a pretty school girl.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: That’s what I tell Mrs. Oldfield, but she’s such a woman; she’s set her heart on it, and so she’s been at him till he’s consented to the arrangement.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: What arrangement?

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Why, his marriage.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: His marriage! With whom?

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: It’s quite a seeret, but I don’t mind telling you; with Jessy—

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Nonsense! I tell you it’s impossible, it can’t be done! you don’t know what you’re talking about. I’ll not allow it (goes up and looks off, R.)

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Oh! oh! I smell a rat. Well, at any rate, don’t say a word! (aside) I’ve put my foot in it nicely. (aloud) The fact is, Mrs. Oldfield and I thought it would be a nice match.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: (comes down L.) A nice match indeed! Depend on it, Mr. Wylding will never venture on such a step, though there’s no saying what men will do now-a-days. (aside) I wonder whether there is anything in it.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: I certainly should like to see him married to Jessy, but quite think he would find a more suitable match in Mrs. Tiverton.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Pray, don’t mix me up in any way with Mr. Wylding.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Certainly not. (aside) A nod’s as good as a wink! (aloud) I’m sure I’d no idea—I should have been delighted to have seen my friend so blessed.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Blessed with whom?

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: With you. I’m sure he admires you very much.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: My dear Major, pray don’t let that thought enter your head. Mr. Wylding and I must ever be to each other what we are; my destiny is fixed, I have no longer a hand or a heart to give. I regret to say, that a letter received to-day will oblige me to shorten my visit to you.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: I hope not.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: I’m very sorry to leave, but I’ve some important business at Doctors’ Commons that demands my presence.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Indeed. (aside) That sounds odd.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: My dear Major, should the opportunity present itself, I should be obliged if you would let Mr. Wylding know my sentiments respecting him. (aside) He shall know my opinion in another way. It must be nearly time for our returning to your house. I’ll go and find Mrs. Oldfield.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: There’s no hurry, I dare say; we shan’t go for this hour to come.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: At any rate, I’ll get ready to go. (aside) I’m quite flurried by this intelligence. Exit into cottage, L.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: She’s a charming woman, but, I should say, knows her way about. But that Master Percy’s a bit of a flirt; I must talk to him.

div.stage: Enter PERCY WYLDING, L. U. E.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Hallo, Major! what, alone? Well, I think we’ve had a pleasant afternoon.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Charming! How could it be otherwise in such a spot, with such company? Come, I want to talk to you a little about Jessy.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: I’m all attention.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: You quite understand my sentiments respecting her marriage.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: (aside) An old fox—he’s at the same game as his wife. This is funny.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: For certain reasons, I’ve a particular wish not to be in any way mixed up in the affair. The fact is, that were I to interfere about it, I should appear to be behaving unhandsomely by my co-trustee, who is anxious that his cub of a son should marry her. So, you see, I want to be able to say that I know nothing about the matter.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p:
Yes, yes, I quite understand; if Jessy wishes to
marry, you would not withhold your approbation, but do not wish to be consulted. (they rise)

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Precisely. Let Mrs. Oldfield conduct the whole affair; I wish to know nothing about it. My co-trustee will be displeased; but, really, his son is such a lout, I couldn’t bear to see the dear girl so sacrificed. He only cares for her money.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: The deuce take him; he shan’t have her. I’ll take care of that.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: I know you will—I’m delighted; but, I say, old fellow, I must tell you that I thought you a little too sweet on the fair widow.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: What widow?

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: That’s a good ’un! Why, Mrs. Tiverton; but you don’t stand any chance there, I can tell you—she’s booked.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Booked! Who’s booked?

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Why, Mrs. Tiverton.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: What on earth do you mean?

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Why, I mean that Mrs. Tiverton is going to be married again; I’m certain of it.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: The deuce she is. But who told you, and what’s your authority?

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: The best possible—Mrs. Tiverton, herself.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: The devil! this is a pretty go.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: You seem taken aback.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Do I?—oh, dear, no. But how came you to be admitted into her confidence on the subject?

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Why, the truth is, I rather joked her about your attentions.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: What did she say?

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Was up in a moment, and said that her mind was made up; and, in fact, seemed to have very strong feeling on the subject.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: An aversion to me, eh?

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: I didn’t say that exactly. She says her plans are fixed for life –that she has given her hand and heart: between ourselves, I suspect—

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: What do you suspect?

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: That our fair widow, is only a widow bewitched.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: What do you mean?

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Why, she talks about having important business in Doctors’ Commons. Now, what can that be but a divorce affair?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: No doubt you’re right: she’s getting up a divorce.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Well, at any rate that’s no affair of ours—dismiss her from your mind. I must be off: I say, be less attentive to Mrs. Tiverton, or you’ll get yourself talked about, and that would be awkward under your circumstances—good-bye for the present (aside, going) I think I’ve managed that very well. Exit into cottage, L.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Under my circumstances! What does he mean by that? She can’t have told him our real position, and taken him into her confidence that he might break the matter of the divorce to me. The way that bridegroom has been going on with her is decidedly suspicious; certainly there must be something between them, for I saw him talking to her so earnestly, seeming to implore her forbearance, and she was giving him a good lecture I should say at least, so it seemed to me from the distance. Oh, here he is; altogether I’ve got plenty on my hands.

div.stage: Enter EGERTON, R. U. E., comes down, L.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: I was just coming to look for you.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: L. What a charming place you have here.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: R. Not bad for a bachelor’s box: it is very odd I can’t help fancying that I’ve met you before.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Very probably: I’ve been a great traveller.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Yes, to be sure, it must be; but, no, it’s impossible; you can’t be the man I take you for.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Why impossible?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Because he’s dead. I was on board a Mediterranean steamer with him—we were run down in the night –in the general confusion I saw your likeness struggling in the water –I tried to assist him in vain.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Mr. Wylding, I should indeed be ungrateful, were I not both to recognize and thank you. I am the identical man you attempted to help on that fearful night.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Are you indeed. I’m deuced glad you were saved.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: I would have called myself to your recollection be- fore, but that I wished for an opportunity of doing so privately, as I do not wish my wife to know of that terrible affair at present: I am sure I may rely on your not alluding to it in any way.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Certainly.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: That was indeed a heavy night for me –in it I lost a treasure.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: What do you mean?

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: My wife.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Did you? It was an awful affair. I was lucky enough to save one lady: I held her up in the water till the boat of the Messageries picked us up. Her sole anxiety was to know the fate of her husband. I met her again last year in Switzerland, and would have called on her, but she was living in such seclusion that she declined seeing me: in fact, she was not as grateful as she might have been.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: What was her name?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Mrs. Thornton.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Mrs. Thornton saved? Impossible!

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Nothing of the kind; she was saved by me.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Mr. Wylding, for mercy’s sake, you are jesting or dreaming! Mrs. Thornton, my wife, escaped a watery grave?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Mrs. Thornton your wife? impossible! is your name Thornton, then?

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: It was; I changed it to inherit property.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Stop; you don’t mean to say that you’ve married again under the impression that you lost her on that night?

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: I have indeed.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Good gracious! what’s to be done?

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: What indeed! are you sure that you are not mistaken?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: I’ll tell you who can verify my statement.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Who is that?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Mrs. Tiverton. She and Mrs. Thornton were acquainted; I’ve seen them together.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: I must see her instantly, and ask her; but for heaven’s sake don’t breathe a word to a soul. I’ll see you again to-night, and consult with you as to what’s to be done. I must act promptly. (sinks into a seat) And as a man of honour I ought to quit this place at once.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: No, no, wait till you’ve seen Mrs. Tiverton, at any rate.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: No, I’d better fly immediately; as to Mrs. Tiverton, she already knows too much, for to tell the truth, I’ve had to ask her to be silent. Ah! not a word now.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: C. Mamma has gone home. Why, Harry, what’s the matter?

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Nothing, my love, nothing; I’m only a little out of sorts.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: R. I don’t think that the sea air quite agrees with him.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: I’m sure he’s ill—very ill—is he not, Mr. Wylding?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Well, I should say that he was not very well.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: I’m all right now; I’ll get a glass of water.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Let me get you one.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: No, dearest, I’ll go myself. Exit into cottage, L.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: What can be the matter with him? Tell me, Mr. Wylding, what has he been talking about? I heard him mention Mrs. Tiverton’s name.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Did he? well, yes, I believe he did. (aside) Confound him! I don’t know it’s very odd and very awkward.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: What is odd and awkward?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Why, his being taken ill.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: But you know the cause of this attack—I’m sure you know.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Don’t you be sure of anything! I really can’t say what the cause of his illness may be.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: My dear Mr. Wylding, as a valued friend, I implore you, tell me—don’t keep me in suspense.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: I can’t—I’ve nothing to disclose. (aside) I wish to goodness I knew myself. Go and see how he is by this time. Exit ALICE into cottage, L. This is a pretty imbroglio—how’ll they get out of it? I don’t see what’s to be done; deuced pleasant. He was just going to let the cat out of the bag about himself and my wife. That girl is right; there must be something very queer; I must have it out. I’ll teach my wife a lesson; I’ll show her I’m not to be made a fool of. This is a little too strong; I must be cool and cautious. I’ll wash my hands, and drop in next door. Exit into cottage, L.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: I can’t be bear to be alone with her, to think that I should be deceiving her. Her anxious looks when she thinks me ill, go to my heart. I must see Mrs. Tiverton at once; should she confirm Wylding’s intelligence, I must leave the place, and for ever.

div.stage: Re-enter MRS. TIVERTON from cottage, L.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Mrs. Tiverton, one word, if you please.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: What is the matter? I trust, Mr. Egerton, that you do not doubt my honour. I have given you my word to keep your secret, that is enough.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: I was not about to refer to the conversation we have just had. I feel the justice of your remarks, and that I deserve your censure, but I must implore you to be prudent.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: (aside) Hallo! there they are again.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: When I begged you not to breathe a word about it to Mr. Wylding, I did not—

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Pray, compose yourself, Mr. Egerton. Mr. Wylding, indeed, do you think I’m mad? I’d as soon print it on a thousand handbills, and distribute them through the county.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: (aside) That’s pleasant for me!

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: You know him then?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Indeed I do!

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: He’s a kind-hearted fellow, but his discretion—

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Mr. Egerton, you may spare yourself the trouble telling me anything about Mr. Wylding.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: (aside) I’d better finish dressing. Exit.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: I was about to speak on a subject which interests me, I believe you have resided for some time in Switzerland?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Yes, I passed two summers there!

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: You were there last summer?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: I was.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Did you happen to know a Mrs. Thornton?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Intimately; hers was a remarkable story, but perhaps you knew her, and have heard it?

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Yes! I know her; that is to say, I’ve heard the story—you refer to the loss of the steamer and her escape?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Yes, it was most miraculous!

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: I never heard the particulars.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: There I’m a little at fault, for to tell the truth, the nervous shock had upset her so very much she could not bear to refer to it; indeed, wished to keep the fact of her escape a secret.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: What could have been her motive for that?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Why, she had a husband from whom she wished to escape, and availed herself of the report of her supposed death to do so, but—

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Impossible! You must be mistaken; tell me, is she in England?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: I’m sure she’s not. When last I heard from her she was at Vevey; she has no ties in England, and does not care about it.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: (aside) Poor dear, solitary Clarisse, her mind must be gone. How am I to act?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: You seem very much interested about Mrs. Thornton. I owe her a letter; when I write, I shall inform her of your enquiries.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Not for the world. Pray, Mrs. Tiverton, I implore you, do not in any way refer to me. I shall shortly communi- cate with her.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: My dear sir, you are very tragical over it; if I were not aware that such a thing is impossible, I should say you were a discarded lover, or something of that sort; the very idea makes me laugh!

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Mrs. Tiverton, pray do not laugh; you little know the agony of my soul, the utter wretchedness of my feelings; a few hours ago I was the happiest of men; but now am the most miserable.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Indeed! I trust that there is nothing unpleasant between you and your wife!

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: May I confide to you— ALICE appears at L. door, and starts back on seeing them. —one more secret; will you solemnly promise to me, to keep it inviolate till I give you permission to reveal it?

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: (coming forward) No Sir, you shall confide no secret to any woman but me.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Alice! you here.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Yes, Alice is here, to receive this secret from you, which she could have learned had she been willing to have stooped to the baseness of listening. As you were about to confide to this lady, you can have no objection to my sharing your confidence, and I’m sure she would prefer my doing so.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: My dear Mrs. Egerton, I assure you that the confidence was quite volunteered by your husband; I was merely speaking of a lady—

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Mrs. Tiverton let us return at once to the lodge. Come, Alice.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: I’m quite ready. (aside) She is so cool, that I can’t make her out, but there must be something wrong—I will be satisfied !

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: I say, good people, this is all very fine, but we ought to have been at the lodge an hour ago; Mrs. Oldfield will think we’ve eloped, or something ; why, what’s the matter? (aside) I’m afraid the cloud has burst !

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Nothing particular! I’ve only interrupted a very interesting, if not agreeable, téte-a-téte between Mrs. Tiverton and my husband.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: My dear Mrs. Egerton, how very absurd, we were only talking about a friend whom——

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Mrs. Tiverton––

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: My dear sir, pray allow the lady to finish her defence.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Defence of what, pray? Don’t carry this folly too far! As a young, inexperienced married woman, your silly fit of jealousy is excusable, but I must beg you will accept my utter disclaimer as to there being any sort of foundation for it, as far as I am concerned. I hope to meet you at tea, when you’ve arranged your little matrimonial difference. I must trouble you, Mr. Wylding, to shew me the way out.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: With the greatest possible pleasure—I’m sure that is—I mean—(aside) Confound her, she quite baffles me with her coolness! Exit into cottage, L., with MRS. TIVERTON.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: My dear Alice, you really are unreasonable? I assure you it is unjust, both to Mrs. Tiverton and myself.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Pray don’t tax your powers of invention any further. Can you deny that you were going to make her a confidante, and tell her something you have been carefully concealing from me? But, come, Harry, why should you keep any secret from me? Pray tell me what is vexing and annoying you so? I will do all I can to soothe and comfort you.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Alice—Alice—impossible! I am a wretched, lost man, and when I look on you—

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: L. Are you not coming? I must do the honours as long as my guests remain. I’m afraid we’re very late.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: R. Mr. Wylding, take Alice home; I will rejoin you shortly, at present I feel quite unequal to say more. Crosses and exit, L.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p:
I am petrified! You still say you know nothing,
Mr. Wylding, about the reason of my husband’s strange behaviour?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: I said nothing of the sort. I said I could tell you nothing.

sp.speaker: Alice.
Percy.
sp.p: No, no, really you are wrong there; I am sure there can be no secret between them. (aside) I’m shuffling now.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Of my husband’s former feeling towards her there can be no doubt at any rate, for he has her miniature in his possession.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: The deuce he has! how do you know that?

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Because I’ve seen it, at least I’m nearly certain of it. Imagine any one with a spark of feeling for her late husband behaving as she does. How long has he been dead?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: I’m not aware that he is dead.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Not dead! what do you mean?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Mrs. Egerton, Mrs. Egerton, you are carried away by your feelings. I assure you Mrs. Tiverton is a lady of good connections and irreproachable conduct. (aside) I wish I could say that from my heart. (aloud) Why, bless me, I ought to know! you quite perplex me.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Not dead! what do you mean? That she is only passing herself off as a widow? Mamma must know this—I’ll go to her at once. She’s some adventuress—no one is safe! She’s been flirting with every one since she’s been here, from Freddy to papa. Exit, L.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Mrs. Egerton—Alice—stop! What am I to do— what can I say? I can’t say she’s my wife without her consent —I can’t get hold of her to see her alone. I never did in all my life know such a complication! How will they get out of it? Exit, L.

div.head: ACT III.
SCENE.
div.stage: Drawing Room at Oldfield Lodge; card tables; sofa; chairs; chess table, L,; windows in flat; open terrace, and distant sea view. Moonlight.
MAJOR OLDFIELD and MRS. TIVERTON at chess, L.—MRS. OLDFIELD at work, R., PERCY WYLDING hanging over her chair— JESSY and ALICE at table, C., looking at book—FREDDY talking to EGERTON at sofa.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Well, Mr. Wylding, how did Freddy take your intelligence?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Very well—said he was quite ready to fall into my views.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: You didn’t let him know that I had any hand in the matter?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: No further than by saying I was in possession of your sentiments on the subject, and that seemed quite to satisfy him.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Dear boy! always docile and obedient to me. How did you get on with Jessy?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: First rate; she seemed a little taken aback at first, but I soon brought her to reason.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: I really think you must be a wizard; the manner in which you can get one out of every difficulty is marvellous.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: You can do anything in this world if you’re only prompt, and strike while the iron’s hot; that’s the way to rule the world.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: That’s check-mate for me. Come Percy, you must try to vindicate the honour of our village: Mrs. Tiverton is carrying off everything, including our hearts.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Major Oldfield!

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: I’m dumb.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: I’m far too much a novice to break a lance with Mrs. Tiverton — I should soon get the worst of it. (aside) I wish I could see her alone, I’d tax her with her behaviour. (aloud) It’s a shame to be indoors such a lovely night; don’t you think a stroll in the garden by moonlight would be delightful, Mrs. Tiverton?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: A year or two ago I should have jumped at such a proposal, but my day of romance is passed.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Don’t say so. (aside) I want to speak to you.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: (observing them, aside) There’s some secret under- standing between those two: I’ll watch them.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: I wish you’d come out, Mrs. Tiverton.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: (aside) What can he have to say? (aloud) I’m very comfortable here, thank you, Mr. Wylding.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: (aside to WYLDING) I say, remember what I said to you; don’t you be too attentive in that quarter.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Why not; I can take care of myself.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Yes; but consider others. (aloud) Jessy would like a turn in the moonlight, no doubt.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: You’re right. Here Freddy, why don’t you take Jessy out for a moonlight stroll.

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.p: I shall be delighted—will you come, Jessy?

sp.speaker: Jessy.
sp.p: With pleasure.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Freddy, don’t stay out too long—think of your cold.

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.p: All right, mother.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: (to WYLDING) What are you sending them out together for?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Part of my plan.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: I meant you to take her.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Mrs. Tiverton and I are going directly.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: (aside) Then I’ll go too. (aloud) I must come and play propriety.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: By all means. I don’t at all approve tete-á-tete wanderings by moonlight.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: I should say they were dangerous; at least, I should be afraid of them.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Major Oldfield!

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: I mean for people like Freddy, with bad colds.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: I don’t know that: I dare say the cool night air will be tempered in his case. Come, Mrs. Tiverton: now, my dear Major, you really ought not to come, ought he, Mrs. Oldfield? Why your last attack of gout was brought on by moonlight (aside) and a little iced punch thrown in.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Major Oldfield is always so imprudent, I never attempt to guide him.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Imprudent, nonsense—I’m no such thing—I’m as hearty as a buck.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: (aside) And as obstinate as a rhinoceros.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: I shall not wait while you settle the point as to the prudence of accompanying me: you can follow when you’ve made up your minds. Exit, c.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: I’m coming directly.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: (holding him) My dear fellow, don’t go doing moonlight walks with a pretty woman. I don’t suspect you of being a flirt, but really your conduct—

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: My dear Major, you’re quite mistaken; let me go, I want to say a few words in private to Mrs. Tiverton.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: The deuce you do; well, I do call that imprudent, I do indeed. Well, at any rate I’ll come into the garden with you.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: (aside) Confound him, what a bore: but not without your hat—go and get your hat.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: I’ve my smoking cap in my pocket. (puts it on) There, I’m quite ready.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: (aside) We can soon tire him. Very well, come on, Exeunt, C.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Alice, won’t you come out?

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: No, thank you, I’m rather tired; but, pray, don’t let that keep you in.

div.stage: PERCY WYLDING appears at window, c.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Here, Egerton, you’re wanted to settle a dispute between the Major and Mrs. Tiverton, about Venus or Saturn, or some heavenly body. I know nothing about them; so I can’t decide,

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: I’ll come directly.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Come along. Exit EGERTON, C., as PERCY is following.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Mr. Wylding, will you oblige me by remaining here a few moments.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Oh, certainly. (aside) What a bore, just as I had contrived to get Egerton out to absorb the Major. (aloud) Well, what is it?

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: I am going to repeat the question I have already put to you, and prefer doing so in my mother’s presence.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Alice, what can you have to ask? What does all this mean?

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: My dear mother, I regret to say that I have reason to distrust my husband’s truth and honour.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Oh, Mrs. Egerton!

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Good gracious, how very shocking; but I’ve always told you my opinion of a love match.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Love match, indeed! he never really loved me— for, long before, he had fallen into the snares of a practised coquette.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Whom do you mean?

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Mrs. Tiverton.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: No, no; really, you are mistaken.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Can you deny that she is my rival?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Most decidedly I can.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: How, then, do you account for their conduct?

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: What conduct?

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: My dear mamma, I surprised my husband and Mrs. Tiverton, yesterday, at Mr. Wylding’s, and unwillingly overheard enough of their conversation to convince me that she, not I, has his confidence, and the mastery of his heart.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: My love, how monstrous. Why, they only met yesterday.

sp.speaker: Alice.
