sp.p: There you are mistaken, mamma; they are old friends, as Mr. Wylding knows and can testify.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Well now, really, they are only travelling acquaintances.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: How very extraordinary that Mrs. Tiverton should not have stated that she knew him.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Oh, one can’t state everything at once.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Wherever there’s concealment, Mr. Wylding, depend on it, there’s something wrong; and ’tis so easy to explain—

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: I beg your pardon. Never explain anything, unless you want to get deeper into the mess. You may do wrong, and get off very well; but if you’re such a fool as to explain, even when you’ve done right, you’re sure to be put in the wrong. Never explain.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: That may be a very good maxim, but I do not agree to it. I insist on an explanation as regards this Mrs. Tiverton; you know all about her—I’m sure you do. Now prove yourself our friend and hers, by stating all you can in her defence; or, if she be, as I suspect, an adventuress, pray tell us.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: How, can you ask me to do such a thing? Now be reasonable.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: I am perfectly reasonable; you see how calm I am. I have resolved to keep so, in order to be fit for the trial that awaits me.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Whatever your trial may be, Mrs. Tiverton is not the cause of it, I’m sure.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Then, why does my husband make her his confidante?

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: His confidante! Really, Mr. Wylding, this does require clearing up. Mrs. Tiverton leaves us to-morrow, but I must demand an explanation before she goes.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: No, dear mamma, don’t you speak to her; leave me to do so, first, at any rate. She is coming; leave us alone.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: My dear, would it not be better for your mother to speak?

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: No, mamma, I should prefer doing so myself.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Well, my love, as you please. Exit, R.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Now, Mrs. Egerton, let me say a few words to Mrs. Tiverton.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: No, sir; certainly not. If you would have me believe in your sincerity, allow me to settle this affair in my own way. Since you will not explain it, Mrs. Tiverton must; so, leave us alone.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Very well; what’s to be done? Exit, c.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Star-gazing is all very well, but it is slightly chilly, so I’ve come for my shawl. I wonder you don’t join the astronomers, Mrs. Egerton, it’s very interesting.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: So I should think; but I have more interesting matter on hand. I wish to speak to you, Mrs. Tiverton, if you will spare me a few moments.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: By all means; but I trust you are not going to carry on that absurd scene commenced at Mr. Wylding’s.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: It may strike you as absurd; but I’m not a case-hardened woman of the world. I cannot have iny feelings outraged, and bear it with stoical indifference. If I didn’t love my husband, I could bear his neglect.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: I’m sorry to hear that he neglects you; I thought him the most devoted; I really think you fancy it.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Mrs. Tiverton, you cannot think so –it is impossible. You are in possesson of some secret that he is withholding from me. Give me a full explanation, and I shall be satisfied.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: A full explanation of what?

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: The subject of your conversation, this evening, at Mr. Wylding’s.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Mrs. Egerton, you have made a mistake in demanding an explanation of me; if you require one, ask it of your husband.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: This is trifling; you know that I have a right to ask you.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Well, really! as I said before, I must refer you to Mr. Egerton: I cannot betray confidence reposed in me.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: (rises) Confidence reposed in you! How dare he confide in you any secret he has kept from his wife—and how can you accept such a trust?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: You are young and inexperienced. Come, come; let me advise you, as a friend, to calm yourself: ask your husband, for I assure you I cannot think of doing so.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Her coolness will drive me mad. I must say, Mrs. Tiverton, your conduct in this matter places you in a very suspicious light.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: I have been too long, my dear, the victim of misrepresentation, to mind being once more a subject of it. Be advised by me; your husband loves you. Do not let foolish jealousy mar your happiness—confide in him.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: You only exasperate me– I will know the truth. If you will not tell me, I will expose the whole affair—I’ll denounce you both.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Denounce us both! What do you mean?

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Mean! That you have stolen him from me; that he loved you before he ever saw me.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: (aside) This is a pretty situation. (aloud) Mrs. Egerton, you are wronging one who is your friend.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Friend, indeed! Is it the part of a friend to act as you have done? No! you are my foe, and soon shall the mask be torn off; I’ll go and find my husband, and bring him face to face with you. (going, R.) Oh, Mrs. Tiverton, if you really don’t love him, please give him back to me, for I do love him dearly. Exit, R. door.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Give him back to her indeed! This comes from having a secret. I told him that he should have let her know everything.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: I declare I’ve got quite chilled. You were wise to come in, Mrs. Tiverton.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: I’m always prudent; but in this present instance was actuated by the necessity of preparing for my departure in the morning.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p:
I’m sorry you’re obliged to go. This has been
such a flying visit, we must make you promise to return to us, mustn’t we, my dear?

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Mrs. Tiverton is in possession of my sentiments respecting her. (aside) How I should like to tell her what I think of her; but it may be all right, I mustn’t interfere yet.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: I shall say good-night and good-bye. (crosses to c.)

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: I shall be down before you leave.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: I must be off by the first train; my maid will secure me a cup of coffee, so pray don’t disturb yourself; once more good-bye. (crosses to R.)

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Well, good-bye, and remember, if you are ever within one hundred and fifty miles of us, and don’t favour us with your presence, we shall consider you are acting shabbily.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Major Oldfield !

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: I shall be sure to come. Exit, R. door.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Charming creature! Delightful woman! Heigho!

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Major Oldfield, you really are getting sentimentally imbecile.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: That’s civil, upon my word—I’m off to bed.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: I just wish to make one remark, Major Oldfield: I hope you are at any rate pleased with the turn affairs have taken with respect to Jessy.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: I am certainly pleased; I can’t quite make him out—but to tell you the truth, I always thought you had an eye on Jessy for Freddy.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Freddy, indeed, dear boy! I believe he was a little sweet on her once; but as soon as he knew that I was opposed to the match, he gave it up immediately.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: (aside) I wonder how much she bought him off for. (aloud) Oh, indeed I say Mrs. Oldfield, who are the moonlight wanderers in the garden? they look to me very much like Freddy and Jessy.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: What are you knocking your head against now? Freddy has gone to bed with a bad cold.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Gone to bed, indeed! Here, Freddy—Hallo! your mother wants you.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Really, one would think you’d been been drinking. Freddy is not there, I’m sure?

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.p: Here I am.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Freddy, how imprudent to be out so late with your cold.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: And with your cousin?

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Major Oldfield, don’t be absurd. Freddy, what can you possibly have to say to Jessy?

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.p: Mother, how can you ask such a question?

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: My dear Jessy, you must be more circumspect even with regard to Freddy; Mr. Wylding will not like it; and people will think you a coquette under your circumstances.

sp.speaker: Jessy.
sp.p: What circumstances, dear aunt?

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Why, being engaged to Mr. Wylding, you must not act quite so freely as heretofore.

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.p: Engaged to Wylding! Ha, ha! that’s capital—the idea of my Jessy being engaged to Wylding.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: What do you mean, sir?

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.p: Oh, I know all about it; Wylding has told me your wishes, and I comply willingly; and so does Jessy in all respects but one.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: What are you talking about?

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.p: I know you both wish me to marry Jessy.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O. | Major
sp.p: Marry Jessy?

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Who told you so?

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.p: Wylding, of course; he said that both you and my mother wished it.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: I never did—what effrontery—it’s false, sir.

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.p: He said that, as you did not wish to appear to have any hand in it, I’d better run away with her; and I’d have done it like a bird, only Jessy wouldn’t hear of such a thing.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Dear girl! (aside) Wouldn’t she though?

sp.speaker: Jessy.
sp.p: You don’t think, my dear uncle, I would requite your kindness by such an act of disrespect?

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: I’m sure you wouldn’t; do you think she would, my dear?

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Certainly not; but really, this Mr. Wylding, what a viper he is.

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.p: No, no; I’m sure he misunderstood you; he’s a noble fellow.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: I’ll noble fellow him; I’ll go and find him this instant; I never was in such a rage. Come with me, Freddy; have this explained before I sleep. Exit with FREDDY, R.

sp.speaker: Jessy.
sp.p: Dear uncle, you are not displeased with me?

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: No, love, very much the reverse go to your room now, all will be well: we shall hear what Mr. Wylding has to say; you shall soon know. Exit JESSY, R. If I can find out that Wylding did make a mistake, I shall only be too glad to consent to the marriage.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Why, Ally, my love, what is the matter?

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Papa, dear! dear papa! (falls into his arms)

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: My own darling, what ails you?

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: My dearest father, pity your poor girl; I have been deceived, my husband loves another.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Impossible, my child, you are the victim of a delusion.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: No, no, papa; it is too true; I have proof.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Then I’ll find the rascal, and demand an explanation.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Not yet, dear papa, let us wait till we can bring his falsehoods home to him.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: How do you propose doing so, my love?

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: To-night it is his intention to leave the house, but I’ll prevent him.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Can it be?

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Yes; and I’ve reason to believe that Mrs. Tiverton will be the partner of his flight.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Mrs. Tiverton! My dear girl, you must be dreaming.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Father, I am not; I have been almost heart-broken at the discovery, but now I only wish to be convinced of my husband’s falsehood and my own desolation; then I shall feel I have been properly punished for my wilfulness, and I know that you, dear papa, will pity me as you have forgiven me. Happy in your love I will endeavour to forget him, and devote myself to you and dear mamma.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: My darling, come to your father’s arms.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: I hear my husband coming; don’t see him now, come with me. I will tell you my scheme for exposing this treachery and deceit. Exit with MAJOR, R.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Well, don’t bother me about it; I can’t help it, if your mother don’t know her own mind. I repeat, she told me she wished you to marry Jessy.

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.p: She declares she never said a word to you about it.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: I shouldn’t like to tell you what I think she is.

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.p: She says you professed yourself a great admirer of Jessy.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: So I am –so is everybody; but we can’t all marry Jessy.

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.p: My mother says I shan’t –she has been talking to me. I can tell you—

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Where is your mother ?

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.p: When we didn’t find you at home, she went off in a huff to bed, I suppose.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: I was only in the stables; but come, you go to bed, too, and sleep on it. You’ll find all will be right; your mother is not likely to oppose you very long, you know. Now be off, there’s a good boy.

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.p:
By-bye, then. I’m sent to bed, am I? I shan’t go—I’ll go and talk to my mother. Well, if you don’t
redeem your word, and get me married to Jessy as you promised, I shall never believe in you any more—good night. Exit, R. door.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Good night. Who would have the guiding of such a family? It’s lucky I’m pretty cool, or they’d get themselves into no end of scrapes. I’ve agreed to help poor Egerton out of this difficulty by driving him over to the Junction to meet the mail train. What a blow-up there will be in the morning. Enter EGERTON, L. Ah! here you are, ready, eh?

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: My kind friend, I am, though it breaks my heart to think of my poor Alice’s desolation; be kind to her. Write at once and let me know how she bears the shock. I have written her a letter that will explain all.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Then give it me I’ll deliver it when you’re clear off. I’ll drive the trap to the end of the lane, and wait there; now don’t you linger here. Give me the letter.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: I had it here—no—I must have left in my dressing room. I’ll get it at once. Exit, L.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Yes, look sharp.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Mr. Wylding, you still here?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Yes. I’m just going.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Going?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Yes, I’m going for a drive.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: At this time of night?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Yes, I often do it I like it—it’s so quiet!

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Very quiet, indeed, but rather too much so not to excite suspicion. Mr. Wylding, I have discovered your plot, in spite of your admirable powers of deception.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: My plot?

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Yes! Can you deny that you are a party to the elopement of my husband and Mrs. Tiverton?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: I’ll be hanged if I am!

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Can it be possible that you are the dupe of both ? yes, it must be so; you cannot be so base as to betray the confidence of those who love you so dearly, as we all do. But be no longer the victim of their arts, you are deceived by both—they quit this house by different routes and at different times; but it is nothing more than a ruse, they have no doubt settled the place of meeting.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Can it be—no—yes of course I see it all! I’ve been made a fool of? You’re right—I’ll see him further before I drive him to the train.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Then you were about to assist him in his flight?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Yes, he’s a bad man; you and I are two victims; he’ll be here directly.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Let me speak to him alone.Exit WYLDING, C.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: It is hard to leave her thus! but it must be done. (going—meets ALICE)

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: R. One word, sir, before you thus abandon me. What are your reasons for this step?

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: L. Alice! pray spare this agony, if you ever loved me.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: If I ever loved you? Can you doubt my love? Cold, wicked, perfidious though you be, you must have discovered that I loved with all the truth and ardour that a woman can love!

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Alice, you torture me and drive me mad! Did you ever have cause till now to doubt my love?

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: ’Till now? What a question for the wife of little more than a month, to be asked. I never could have believed you false till I saw your conduct with that woman.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: What woman?

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Mrs. Tiverton!

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: No, Alice—no, by heaven you are wrong! you are doing a great injustice in suspecting her, she is entirely blameless.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Of course, you defend her with an ardour worthy of a better cause. Mr. Egerton, I know all.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Alice, you are mad !

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: You will make me so! Can you disguise your passion for her? You have known her before; if all were as it ought to be, why did you keep the fact of your being acquainted a secret? Why did you have a clandestine interview with her? above all, why are you about to leave this house surreptitiously at this hour?

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: I have been wrong, but not guilty; I have deceived you.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Oh! you admit it?

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Yes—but only in a trifling matter; when I wooed and won you, I was guilty of one piece of deceit. I concealed from you the fact of my being a widower.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: What could have been your motive for such concealment, but a bad one?

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: No, not a bad but a weak one. I had heard you express a girlish dislike to second marriages. I feared to lose you, or even have the agony of suspense, as to your accepting me; it was love that blinded me, and it was because Mrs. Tiverton knew of my being a widower, that I ignored her as a former acquaintance, and asked her to do me the favour of keeping the fact from you.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Henry Egerton, will you swear to me that this is the whole truth?

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: No, I will not.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Ah!

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: It was the whole truth, but now, alas! there is a terrible revelation to be made. I lost my first wife in an awful manner. We were on our wedding trip, and on coming up from Naples, the steamer which conveyed us was run down. In vain I struggled to save my wife; a fellow-passenger assisted me, but to no purpose; overcome by the waves, I saw my loved wife sink into death, and then lost all consciousness; recovering my senses, I found myself on board a vessel bound for the Levant, by the crew of which I had been saved; on my arrival in Smyrna, I was thrown on a sick bed, and remained an invalid all the winter. After that I returned to Naples, to have my worst fears confirmed, and to learn the details of the calamity that had made me a widower; two or three men alone had escaped. I could not bear any reference to the subject, and I travelled in the East for the recovery of my health, and to try and divert my thoughts from my great affliction. On my return to Europe, I encountered Mrs. Tiverton at Vienna; we were. I assure you, but slightly acquainted, for I was then a prey to the deepest melancholy. I subsequently met you, and sunshine again broke upon my path.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: That miniature, is it not of Mrs. Tiverton?

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Mrs. Tiverton—no. Of my loved lost one; but I must tell you all the truth. Since I have been here, I’ve recognized in Mr. Percy Wylding, my fellow-passenger on board the ill-fated steamer, who had tried to help me, and have learnt from him that my wife was saved.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Was saved, and is still alive, merciful heavens! Do you mean to tell me that you have contracted marriage with me without being assured of her death? This is too much; you have disgraced not only me, but my family; brought ruin and infamy upon us all. Oh! ’tis a hideous dream. Harry, ’tis not true; say that it is not true, that this is but a flimsy artifice to conceal the truth. You wish to fly from me with the woman of your heart, and have invented this subterfuge to deceive me. It is so, Harry, say that it is so!

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Alice, Alice; believe me—

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Ahl no longer call me by that name. I am, you say, nothing to you but your cast-off victim. Oh! Harry, how could you requite my love my devotion?

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Alice, I implore your pardon. I solemnly swear that you are wrong in your suspicion.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: I will know the truth. I will call my father. Ah! you shrink from meeting him.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: I would spare him the misery of this exposure. I would try to shield you from all the consequences of this wretched affair. (going)

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: No, Harry, before my parent you shall proclaim the truth. I desire that you remain here till I call him. O Harry! Exit, R.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Was ever man in so miserable a plight. I cannot bear that her father should hear the tale of misery from my lips; I cannot, will not, face him. I’ll go to Wylding. Rushes out, L. 2 E.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Yes, you may go to him, but you’ll not find him. A nice fellow, that, to try and take me in; it’s fortunate I did not let my feelings run away with me, or I might have believed him, and helped him at once—ah, there’s nothing like being cool and cautious. Who’s here? My precious partner—no doubt, on the watch to see him off. I’ve caught her alone, at last. (retires up)

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: (crossing to L.) I must have left it here—ah, there it is. (takes up shawl) They don’t seem to be gone to bed—how late they are—ah! WYLDING comes forward Mr. Wylding, how you made me jump. I did not expect to find you here.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: R. I dare say not, and wish me further, no doubt; but I’m not so easily bamboozled as you think.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: And who wishes to bamboozle, as you so elegantly express it ?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: I’ve no time for being elegant; I must be plain and straightforward with you.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: What do you mean?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Mean! That your conduct is disgraceful that you not only are ruining your fair fame, but compromising me.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: I must say, this comes well from you, who have abandoned me in the most heartless way.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: I deny it—you forsook me. No, no; you’ll not get your divorce, on the grounds of desertion. Perhaps, you think to irritate me into giving you a pinch, and then you’ll plead cruelty as well.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Get my divorce, indeed. Your behaviour would puzzle me, if I didn’t know your plans. I had intended to have quitted this roof without deigning to notice your conduct, and meant to have denounced you by letter to Major Oldfield. I’ll thwart your designs.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: What designs?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: What designs! Before I answer that question, let me hear what there is in my way of proceeding that so highly incenses you.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: You ask that, do you? Well, then, I’m disgusted at your behaviour with Egerton.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: My behaviour with Mr. Egerton. You’re dreaming.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Oh, dear, no. I’ve watched you both—I overheard you speak of me disparagingly; others have remarked your conduct with him, likewise, and we all agree that it’s disgraceful.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Who dares insinuate anything against me?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Insinuate! I like that. I don’t insinuate; I bring a direct charge. You have known Egerton before, and you have induced him to forsake his young wife, and are prepared to elope with him this very night.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: I must be in a dream, or you must be intoxicated. You said others were convinced of the impropriety of my conduct; name them, at once, I insist?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Well, Mrs. Egerton.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Mrs. Egerton? Poor thing! she is full of the jealousy natural to a young loving heart! I can forgive her; but as to you, I’ve no patience with your folly. This is but a miserable attempt to further your designs upon that poor young girl.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: My designs on what young girl?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Jessy Ashton, whom you are about to marry, when you’ve got rid of me; this is base, indeed!

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: I marry Jessy Ashton? who told you I was going to marry her?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Major Oldfield!

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Then he’s a—

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Spare your invectives, sir; if there is nothing between you and Miss Ashton, pray, why were you talking to her so earnestly in the garden to-day? I saw you!

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Saw me! of course you did. What a blundering old fool that Major is; well, then, I was—I don’t mind telling you, though it’s a secret—I was arranging her marriage with young Oldfield.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: You were? Can I believe you?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Did you ever find me guilty of falsehood?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Never. But, why should I believe you, when you won’t take my word as to my conduct with Mr. Egerton? It is true, he has trusted a secret to me.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Ah! then there is a secret?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: There is, but one which in no way involves my discredit. Will you trust me?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: I will, so far—but how can you clear yourself with others? Your fair fame is compromised by your equivocal position.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Can you not clear it by stating truth respecting me? at least, when I’ve left the house—not before.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: What! tell them you’re my wife, when they know you hate and despise me, and are going in for a divorce?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Going in for a divorce? who says so?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Why, Major Oldfield; and he said, moreover, that you had told him to tell me.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: I believe there must be something in the air of this place that produces insanity! What could have induced the Major to fancy such a thing?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: You told him you had business at Doctors’ Commons. What is it?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: So I have to prove my Aunt Fussell’s will.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Is that all? (aside) What a relief! How, then, am I to act in order to clear up this affair? I must tell them the truth!

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Well, yes, on the whole, I think it would be as well, I see no other way out of this difficulty; but wait till I’m gone.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Very well! but I must now see after that poor devil, Egerton. I must help him out of his scrape !

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: What scrape?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: It’s a deuced awkward affair, and hang me if I know how he’s to get out of it!

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Get out of what?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: You know, about this marriage!

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: What marriage?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: This marriage with Miss Oldfield! didn’t he ask you about a Mrs. Thornton whom you knew in Switzerland?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Yes, yes! well, what of her?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Why—don’t say a word yet but it must soon be known; Egerton’s name was Thornton—that Mrs. Thornton is his wife he thought her drowned, and married again in ignorance of her being saved.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: My dear Percy—I mean, Mr. Wylding—you must be mad; who told him his wife was saved?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: I did!

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: You did? It is true Mrs. Thornton was saved, but not the Mrs. Thornton you imagine her to be, but a poor creature who was so illtreated by her husband that she tried to keep her escape a secret, in order to prevent his finding her, but to no purpose.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: My dear Harriet—I mean Mrs. Tiverton—I am so delighted; but are you sure?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Of course.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Then how could I make such a mistake?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Why, you always take things for granted, and jump at conclusions. I’ve heard Mrs. Thornton say there were two couples of the same name on board the steamer; one Mrs. Thornton was lost, the other escaped.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: But, I say, here’s a dilemma—what’s to be done? I’ve upset the whole family, and have arranged to help Egerton to escape.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: We can soon settle the question of the identity of the lady. I have her letters, and also her photograph; I’ll fetch them this instant.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Pray do; I’m all impatience to relieve poor Egerton. Exit MRS. TIVERTON, R. 2 E. I wonder where he has got to; I must look after him. Exit, c.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: I’ll not steal away; I’m not really guilty; I’ll face the Major, and he shall know the worst.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Come, dear papa, hear it from his own lips. (to EGERTON) I’ve said nothing to my father. Explain to him yourself.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: I have given all the explanation to you; I can say no more.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Now then, Egerton, old boy, such glorious— Halloa! what are you all doing here? thought you were in bed.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: No doubt you did, or you’d not be here. (aside) Is it possible you are still an accomplice in this affair?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Be quiet, will you! (aside to EGERTON) I want to speak to you.

div.stage: Enter MRS. OLDFIELD and FREDDY, R.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Don’t talk to me, sir. I’m sure Mr. Wylding wouldn’t say such a thing.

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.p: Well, there he is ask him.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: I’ve a pretty crow to pluck with you.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Pluck away then.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: I accuse you of deliberate falsehood.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: No, my love, not falsehood—I’ll not believe that. Percy Wylding, did you really misunderstand Mrs. Oldfield in the matter of Freddy and Jessy.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Misunderstand her! certainly not! She asked me to arrange a match between them, and so did you.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: I never said a word, I’ll never believe—

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: You must not impugn our friend’s veracity, my dear, besides, I’ve changed my mind on the matter, and am resolved to consent to the marriage. Freddy, my boy, go and fetch Jessy.

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.p: I’m off like a shot. Exit, c.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Now then, my dear Alice, let’s hear your husband explain this difficulty.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Allow me to say that such a course is quite unnecessary.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Here it is. Oh! I beg pardon for intruding on a family party. Am I de trop?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: No, not at all. You’re the very person I want.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: What are you going to do?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: You’ll see. In the first place, to clear up any misunderstanding that may have arisen respecting Mrs. Tiverton, let me state, that she is not Mrs. Tiverton at all, but Mrs. Percy Wylding, my wife.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: Oh! you rascal.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Good gracious, how incorrect!

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: Is it possible?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Quite true.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Yes, Mrs. Oldfield, I feel that I must explain—

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: My love, particulars reserved for future publication. Are you satisfied. (to ALICE)

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: As far as Mrs. Tiverton is concerned, but are you aware of a statement Mr. Egerton has made to me about his—

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: All right, it’s a mistake of mine.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: What can you mean?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Keep all you’ve heard to yourself; but you shall be satisfied at once. My dear—

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Yes, my love.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: That Mrs. Thornton whom you knew in Switzerland—

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: (aside) What are you doing? Spare my poor Alice this exposure.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: (aside) It’s all right. Will you be quiet. (aloud) Mrs. Thornton, what was she like?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: A very good-natured creature, and answering to the familiar description of fat, fair, and forty.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Tell me quickly. (crossing to MRS. TIVERTON) Was she like this? (produces miniature)

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Not a bit in the world, but we can soon settle that, here is her photograph; you can compare and judge. (gives photograph)

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: But, is this the lady who lost her husband on board the steamer?

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: She thought she had, it was a great shock to her when he turned up. They’re in Switzerland together now.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: (aside to PERCY) Is this the lady you saved?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: (aside) Yes. It seems there were two ladies of that name on board the boat, and that the one I rescued is not your wife.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: But what has Mrs. Thornton to do with this affair?

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Nothing whatever, the little difference between the young couple has arisen through my being mistaken.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: As you always are.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: My dear, I may have been mistaken in times past, but am resolved to be always right for the future.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: If ever you attempt to contradict me, I shall quote the dreadful mess you’ve made about Mrs. Thornton.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Well, then, Alice, love, our hour of misunderstanding has passed.

sp.speaker: Alice.
sp.p: I fear that I have been over hasty; but, as the prudent world would say—I had been so on a former occasion to please you. I have less compunction about my late behaviour: you must admit appearances were against you.

sp.speaker: Eger.
sp.p: Not a word—I have been alone to blame: had I told you the truth, there would have been no ground, at any rate, for your suspicion.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: Well, since all seems so satisfactorily wound up, we may as well say good night. By-the-bye; Percy, my love, you must be off to town at once; you’d better take the mail train, as you’re ready, and save me the trouble of going.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: My dear, I’m going fishing.

sp.speaker: Mrs. T.
sp.p: My love, the fish will excuse your neglect, I’m sure, for one day, and you’ll be so good as to oblige me.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: By all means.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: I always said he’d make a model husband.

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: I hope he won’t carry that too far.

sp.speaker: Mrs. O.
sp.p: Major Oldfield!

sp.speaker: Major.
sp.p: I’m dumb.

sp.speaker: Percy.
sp.p: Come, I think I’ve cleared up everything in a most satisfactory manner, as indeed I always do. Oh! yes! people never need be long in any difficulty where I am, for if I do get my friends into a scrape occasionally, I’m sure to get them safely out of it. I’m always happy to give advice to my friends, I wont say gratis, but they know the usual hour and terms of consultation; and if any of them should find themselves in a difficulty as to where to spend the evening pleasantly, if they’ll only drop in here I shall be most happy to show them how to get out of it.

stage.p: MAJOR. MRS. O. ALICE. EGERTON. MRS. T. PERCY. JESSY. FREDDY..

div.trailer: Printed by Thomas Scott, 1, Warwick Court, Holborn.
